nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; inhabits inner and outer reef slopes from approximately 2 m to 271 m, most commonly encountered below about 30 m. Generally sheltered in caves, crevices, and overhangs in coral and rocky reef structure.

decorPreferences: large, stable rockwork with caves and crevices sized to conceal the full body of an adult (1–2 m)
secured base rocks to prevent collapse from the eel's burrowing and undermining
tightly sealed, weighted lid with no gaps (high escape risk)

diet.notes: Predator of crustaceans (crabs, shrimp) and small to medium fishes in the wild. In captivity has been documented taking pieces of fresh fish, squid, krill, and other meaty marine foods impaled on a long feeding stick, generally offered roughly twice weekly. Newly acquired specimens are often initially reluctant to take prepared foods and may require live ghost shrimp or fiddler crabs as a feed-trigger before transitioning. Hand-feeding is strongly discouraged because of poor eyesight, a powerful bite, and toxic mucus on the skin.

goodWith: large robust fish too big to be swallowed (large angelfish, large tangs, triggers)
other large piscivores in very spacious systems
avoidWith: all crustaceans — shrimp, crabs, hermit crabs, lobsters (natural prey)
other moray eels in tanks under several thousand liters

summary: The Yellow Spotted Moray Eel (Gymnothorax nudivomer) is a large Indo-Pacific reef moray reaching up to ~1.8 m, recognizable by the bright yellow lining of its mouth and a yellow-spotted dark body. Generally regarded as expert-only due to its adult size, escape risk, and toxic skin mucus.
careNotes:
The Yellow Spotted Moray Eel is one of the largest Indo-Pacific morays commonly offered in the trade, with reliable wild lengths of roughly 1.0–1.8 m. Sources broadly agree that it is hardy once feeding and acclimated, but the combination of adult size, aggressive predatory behavior, and pronounced escape risk place it well above typical hobbyist morays and it is generally advised only for highly experienced large-system keepers.

A tightly sealed, weighted aquarium lid with no openings is considered essential — like other morays, this species is widely documented escaping through small gaps. Rockwork should be substantial, with caves large enough to fully conceal an adult body, and base rocks secured against undermining; the eel will rearrange substrate and can collapse unstable stacks.

This species is generally not considered reef-safe. It will prey on any crustacean in the tank and on small fishes, and at adult size can damage corals and invertebrates incidentally while moving through rockwork. Sea Dwelling Creatures lists it as 'reef safe with caution,' while other sources (Reeflex, FishBase context) treat it as unsuitable for reef systems; the typed reefSafe value here reflects the more conservative consensus.

A distinguishing feature is the bright yellow coloration inside the mouth — often interpreted as warning coloration — and the dark body covered in pale yellow to white spots that increase in size toward the tail. Juveniles display a strikingly different yellowish-green ground colour with smaller head spots and are sometimes sold under separate trade names ('Skeletor Moray Eel', 'Starry Moray', 'Dinosaur Moray'). The bold juvenile phase is a major reason this species is heavily traded despite its eventual adult size.

Toxic mucus: the skin secretes a toxin (used by reports as both defensive and feeding-related). Handlers should wear gloves; the species is also reported as poisonous if consumed. This toxicity is in the secreted slime rather than a tank-killing release at death, so it is documented in careNotes rather than flagged via fish.toxicToTank.

Cross-source disagreement noted on tank size: Sea Dwelling Creatures lists 200 US gal (~760 L) as the minimum, while Reeflex lists 2000 L (~530 US gal) and considers it advanced-only. Given confirmed adult lengths of 1.5–1.8 m, the 2000 L figure was retained as the responsible minimum for full adult life; the Sea Dwelling value is consistent with juvenile to sub-adult housing only. This minimum should still be treated as a floor and reviewers may want to revise upward for confirmed adult specimens.

Care-level disagreement: Sea Dwelling Creatures rates 'intermediate'; Reeflex rates 'advanced/only for advanced aquarists'; LiveAquaria does not state a level explicitly. 'Expert' was chosen here to reflect the combination of adult size, toxic mucus, escape risk, and feeding-difficulty risk in newly acquired specimens, all of which push beyond typical 'advanced' marine keeping. Temperament rating also disagreed (Sea Dwelling 'semi-aggressive' vs. Reeflex predatory/solitary); 'aggressive' was chosen as the cautious midpoint consistent with other large piscivorous morays in the data set.

Temperature: FishBase wild preferred range is approximately 19.6–28.1°C; aquarium sources are silent on specifics, so a tighter aquarium-realistic window of 23–27°C was used.

No specific captive lifespan data was found in the sources consulted.

dataStatus is set to needs_review due to: (a) significant spread on tank size between retail and specialist sources (>2x), (b) absent water-parameter detail on multiple aquarium-trade sources, and (c) the unusual toxic-mucus husbandry consideration that warrants explicit human review.
breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented in the sources reviewed. Captive reproduction is generally considered not achieved for large Gymnothorax morays under home-aquarium conditions.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: LiveAquaria eels collection page; lists 'Yellow Spotted Moray Skeletor Eel' as a SKU but did not return a detailed product page in fetch. General eel guidance used: most eels reach 24–36 in, feed mostly on fish and crustaceans, require larger aquariums with hiding places and a well-sealed lid. Confirms the 'Skeletor' trade name applied to this species.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked: taxonomy (Muraenidae, Anguilliformes), max total length 180 cm, depth 2–271 m, preferred temperature 19.6–28.1°C, Indo-Pacific distribution (Red Sea and South Africa to Hawaii/Marquesas, north to Ryukyus, south to New Caledonia), reef-associated, carnivore (trophic level 4.5), toxic mucus, poisonous to eat, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked: tank size ≥2000 L (~440 US gal), temperature 19.6–28.1°C, max length 100–180 cm, care difficulty 'only for advanced aquarists', carnivore (crabs, shrimp, small fish), predatory solitary, not suitable for reef systems, requires very stable rockwork and jump protection, toxic mucus (explicit poison warning).

additional.name: Sea Dwelling Creatures
additional.notes: Cross-checked retail listing: care level intermediate, temperament semi-aggressive, diet carnivore, reef safe 'with caution', minimum tank 200 US gal (~760 L), max length 70 in (178 cm). Trade names: yellowmouth moray, starry moray. Water parameters not stated.

additional.name: Reef Builders
additional.notes: Cross-checked juvenile vs adult color phase: juveniles are yellowish-green with small head/face spots that enlarge toward the tail; adult max ~4 ft (~120 cm shown in article, but other sources extend to 180 cm). Confirms yellow mouth as probable warning coloration, toxic mucus, and trade name 'yellow spotted moray eel'.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive reproduction documented in reviewed sources.
